vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's enforce_for_root parameter controls requirements for enforcing password complexity for the root user. Enable the enforce_for_root setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the root user to use complex passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then if [ -e "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*enforce_for_root/Id" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" else touch "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" cp "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "enforce_for_root" >> "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" # Clean up after ourselves. rm "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: Enabling enforce_for_root in /etc/security/pwquality.conf is a standard PAM configuration change that enforces password complexity for the root user. It follows typical STIG hardening patterns and does not risk lockout or system breakage.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential root password complexity requirement may affect root login if password is not complex enough

reasoning_messages.reasoning:

- If file exists, read it. Check if enforce_for_root line present (maybe commented). If present, uncomment and set value to 1? Typically enforce_for_root = 1. But the recommendation only says enable it, so set to 1. If line not present, add "enforce_for_root = 1". Also if file missing, create file with "enforce_for_root = 1".

But the provided remediation script had enforce_for_root without value, but we should set to 1.

We need safe minimal. So read file. Use sed to replace. Use if line exists with comment, uncomment and set. If exists but not commented, ensure >/=1. If not exists, append. Also ensure newline at end.

But we need to plan, then call review_plan. Not apply (no run_cmd).

So answer content: Step 1: read /etc/security/pwquality.conf. If not exist, create with enforce_for_root = 1 line and newline. If exists, check for line starting with enforce_for_root. If present as '# enforce_for_root', uncomment and set '=1'. If present as 'enforce_for_root', ensure it's '=1'; if other value, modify. If not present, append line.

Also need backup? It's optional. The remediation script backing backup might be good.

But planning: propose to create backup. Replace etc.

After review, if accepted, proceed to actual modification: read file, write modifications, etc. Use sequential commands.

But we cannot chain commands; so each run_cmd single. Might need to read_file => parse locally. But we are ChatGPT, not executing. We'll rely on review.

So send plan with description.
